narrative:
I'm sorry to share that your law school application was rejected because our predictive model estimated a 44.5% chance that you might fail the bar exam on your first try, which is above the threshold we use for admissions decisions. You have some standout strengths, like your impressive LSAT score of 39, which is higher than the typical student's 36.7, and your undergraduate GPA of 3.8, well above the average of 3.21. Your upper-middle family income and full-time study plans also align with or exceed what's common among applicants, and as a white female, your demographic profile is similar to most in our dataset.

However, your school decile rankings from Year 1 and Year 3—both at the bottom 10%—stand out as weaker spots compared to peers who average around the middle of their classes. When the model evaluated your profile, your LSAT score ended up having the single biggest sway in tipping the prediction toward a higher risk of bar failure, even though it's a strong number overall. Right behind that were your low Year 1 and Year 3 deciles, which also nudged the odds toward failure more than any other factors.

Family income came next in influence, followed by your racial background, undergraduate GPA, gender, and full-time status, all of which contributed somewhat to the failure prediction but with less pull than the top items. In essence, while your test scores and grades shine brighter than average, the model weighed your relatively poor class rankings heavily, seeing them as the biggest signals of potential struggle on the bar exam compared to typical students who maintain mid-pack school performance. This combination pushed your risk profile into rejection territory, unlike applicants with more consistent school deciles who often get the green light.

Looking ahead, you can realistically boost your bar exam odds by channeling your high LSAT and GPA strengths into targeted prep, like intensive practice exams and study groups to build the consistent performance your early deciles suggest you've struggled with. Consider supplementing with bar-specific tutoring to address any gaps in legal reasoning that might echo those lower class rankings. With your solid foundation, dedicating focused effort now could flip the script for future applications or even retakes. Keep pushing—you've got more going for you than the model fully credits.
